spec.question: How many home runs did Derek Jeter hit in May 1998?
spec.notes: Phase 1 Q1. 4 HRs: 5/2 vs KCA, 5/6 vs TEX, 5/13 vs TEX, 5/15 vs MIN.

questions.response_text: Derek Jeter hit **4 home runs** in May 1998. That was part of his strong 1998 season, which ended up being one of the best of his career as the Yankees went on to win the World Series.

tool_input.query:
SELECT COUNT(*) AS home_runs
FROM retro.plays p
JOIN retro.games g ON p.gid = g.gid
WHERE p.batter = 'jeted001'
  AND p.hr = 1
  AND g.gametype = 'regular'
  AND EXTRACT(YEAR FROM g.date) = 1998
  AND EXTRACT(MONTH FROM g.date) = 5

checks.name: sql_scalar_matches_answer
checks.detail: headline bolded chunk '**4 home runs**' contains SQL scalar 4

spec.question: How many home runs did Mark McGwire hit in 1998?
spec.notes: Phase 1 Q2. Record-breaking season; #62 off Trachsel 9/8, #70 off Pavano 9/27.

questions.response_text: **70.** Mark McGwire hit 70 home runs in the 1998 regular season, setting what was then the all-time single-season record, breaking Roger Maris's mark of 61 set in 1961.

tool_input.query:
SELECT SUM(hr) AS home_runs
FROM retro.plays p
JOIN retro.games g ON p.gid = g.gid
WHERE p.batter = 'mcgwm001'
  AND g.gametype = 'regular'
  AND EXTRACT(YEAR FROM g.date) = 1998

checks.name: sql_scalar_matches_answer
checks.detail: headline bolded chunk '**70.**' contains SQL scalar 70

spec.question: Who were the best left-handed batters against left-handed pitchers in 1998?
spec.notes: Phase 1 Q3. Top with 150-AB floor: Olerud .375, Vaughn .340, Greer .328, Floyd .325, Vina .324. Good answer surfaces the AB threshold (Andy Fox / dynamism principle, CLAUDE.md §8). Olerud must appear; threshold-surfacing and switch-hitter handling judged on manual review.

questions.response_text:
Here's a great list of lefty-on-lefty mastery in 1998 (100 PA min, sorted by OPS):

| Player | PA | AVG | OBP | SLG | OPS | HR |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Ken Griffey Jr.** | 197 | .299 | .350 | .701 | 1.051 | 21 |
| **Rafael Palmeiro** | 256 | .317 | .375 | .591 | .966 | 15 |
| **John Olerud** | 182 | .375 | .451 | .513 | .964 | 5 |
| **Mo Vaughn** | 220 | .333 | .377 | .586 | .963 | 14 |
| **Larry Walker** | 153 | .321 | .405 | .511 | .916 | 3 |
| **Barry Bonds** | 212 | .281 | .406 | .497 | .903 | 9 |
| **Tony Gwynn** | 173 | .323 | .358 | .534 | .892 | 8 |
| **Will Clark** | 166 | .327 | .373 | .517 | .890 | 5 |
| **Jim Thome** | 166 | .289 | .367 | .500 | .867 | 7 |

**Ken Griffey Jr.** is the runaway leader by OPS — a .701 slugging percentage against lefties is absurd, driven by 21 home runs in just 177 AB. That's the kind of reverse split that makes opposing managers' heads spin.

**John Olerud** is the contact-and-patience standout — a .375 average and .451 OBP against southpaws, though without much over-the-fence pop. **Barry Bonds** took the opposite approach: only .281 average but 39 walks in 212 PA kept his OBP at .406.

**Mo Vaughn**, **Rafael Palmeiro**, and **Tony Gwynn** round out a deep group of lefties who genuinely hurt same-handed pitchers that year.

Worth noting: I used `players.bats='L'` (confirmed left-handed hitters) and `plays.pithand='L'` for pitching hand from play-by-play. Want me to drop the PA threshold, add switch hitters who batted left against lefties, or look at any specific player's full split breakdown?

tool_input.query:
SELECT
    p.batter,
    pl.usename || ' ' || pl.lastname AS player_name,
    COUNT(*) FILTER (WHERE p.pa = 1) AS pa,
    COUNT(*) FILTER (WHERE p.ab = 1) AS ab,
    SUM(p.single + p.double + p.triple + p.hr) AS hits,
    SUM(p.hr) AS hr,
    SUM(p.walk) AS bb,
    SUM(p.k) AS k,
    ROUND(
        SUM(p.single + p.double + p.triple + p.hr)::numeric /
        NULLIF(COUNT(*) FILTER (WHERE p.ab = 1), 0), 3
    ) AS avg,
    ROUND(
        (SUM(p.single + p.double + p.triple + p.hr) + SUM(p.walk))::numeric /
        NULLIF(COUNT(*) FILTER (WHERE p.pa = 1), 0), 3
    ) AS obp,
    ROUND(
        (SUM(p.single) + 2*SUM(p.double) + 3*SUM(p.triple) + 4*SUM(p.hr))::numeric /
        NULLIF(COUNT(*) FILTER (WHERE p.ab = 1), 0), 3
    ) AS slg
FROM retro.plays p
JOIN retro.players pl ON pl.id = p.batter
JOIN retro.games g ON g.gid = p.gid
WHERE
    g.gametype = 'regular'
    AND EXTRACT(YEAR FROM g.date) = 1998
    AND pl.bats = 'L'
    AND p.pithand = 'L'
GROUP BY p.batter, pl.usename, pl.lastname
HAVING COUNT(*) FILTER (WHERE p.pa = 1) >= 100
ORDER BY
    ROUND(
        (SUM(p.single + p.double + p.triple + p.hr) + SUM(p.walk))::numeric /
        NULLIF(COUNT(*) FILTER (WHERE p.pa = 1), 0), 3
    ) +
    ROUND(
        (SUM(p.single) + 2*SUM(p.double) + 3*SUM(p.triple) + 4*SUM(p.hr))::numeric /
        NULLIF(COUNT(*) FILTER (WHERE p.ab = 1), 0), 3
    ) DESC
LIMIT 20;

checks.name: sql_scalar_matches_answer
checks.detail: last run_sql returned 20 row(s) x 11 col(s); not a single-scalar shape

spec.question: Who hit for the cycle in 1998?
spec.notes: Phase 1 Q4. Three cycles: Mike Blowers (5/18 at CHA), Dante Bichette (6/10 vs TEX), Neifi Perez (7/25 vs SLN). All three names should appear; any extra names are likely false positives worth investigating.

questions.response_text:
Three players hit for the cycle in 1998:

| Date | Player | Team | Opponent | Venue |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| May 18 | **Mike Blowers** | Oakland | vs. Chicago White Sox | Chicago |
| June 10 | **Dante Bichette** | Colorado | vs. Texas Rangers | Colorado |
| July 25 | **Neifi Perez** | Colorado | vs. St. Louis Cardinals | Colorado |

A fun footnote: two of the three happened at Coors Field in Denver. Not a coincidence — the thin air and spacious dimensions made it one of the easiest parks in baseball to leg out an extra-base hit, which is exactly what you need for the triple leg of a cycle.

Want me to dig into any of these games further — hit-by-hit breakdown, final score, etc.?

tool_input.query:
SELECT 
    p.gid,
    g.date,
    pl.usename || ' ' || pl.lastname AS player_name,
    bat.batteam,
    SUM(bat.single) AS singles,
    SUM(bat.double) AS doubles,
    SUM(bat.triple) AS triples,
    SUM(bat.hr) AS homers
FROM retro.plays bat
JOIN retro.games g ON bat.gid = g.gid
JOIN retro.players pl ON bat.batter = pl.id
WHERE EXTRACT(YEAR FROM g.date) = 1998
  AND g.gametype = 'regular'
GROUP BY p.gid, g.date, pl.usename, pl.lastname, bat.batteam, bat.batter
HAVING SUM(bat.single) >= 1
   AND SUM(bat.double) >= 1
   AND SUM(bat.triple) >= 1
   AND SUM(bat.hr) >= 1
ORDER BY g.date

            ^

tool_input.query:
SELECT 
    bat.gid,
    g.date,
    pl.usename || ' ' || pl.lastname AS player_name,
    bat.batteam,
    g.visteam,
    g.hometeam,
    SUM(bat.single) AS singles,
    SUM(bat.double) AS doubles,
    SUM(bat.triple) AS triples,
    SUM(bat.hr) AS homers
FROM retro.plays bat
JOIN retro.games g ON bat.gid = g.gid
JOIN retro.players pl ON bat.batter = pl.id
WHERE EXTRACT(YEAR FROM g.date) = 1998
  AND g.gametype = 'regular'
GROUP BY bat.gid, g.date, pl.usename, pl.lastname, bat.batteam, g.visteam, g.hometeam, bat.batter
HAVING SUM(bat.single) >= 1
   AND SUM(bat.double) >= 1
   AND SUM(bat.triple) >= 1
   AND SUM(bat.hr) >= 1
ORDER BY g.date

spec.question: What was the 1998 Yankees' regular-season record?
spec.notes: Phase 1 Q5. 162 games, 114-48, .704. Substrings allow varied phrasings ('114 wins, 48 losses', '114-48').

questions.response_text: The 1998 New York Yankees went **114–48** in the regular season — one of the best records in MLB history. That .704 winning percentage powered them to an AL-best record by a wide margin and set the stage for their dominant World Series run that year.

tool_input.query:
SELECT
  SUM(CASE WHEN wteam = 'NYA' THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) AS wins,
  SUM(CASE WHEN lteam = 'NYA' THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) AS losses
FROM retro.games
WHERE (hometeam = 'NYA' OR visteam = 'NYA')
  AND EXTRACT(YEAR FROM date) = 1998
  AND gametype = 'regular'

checks.name: expected_answer
checks.detail: expected answer '114-48' not found in response

checks.name: sql_scalar_matches_answer
checks.detail: last run_sql returned 1 row(s) x 2 col(s); not a single-scalar shape

spec.question: How many career home runs did Babe Ruth hit?

questions.response_text:
**714 regular-season home runs** — the canonical number, and it matches perfectly.

Ruth played from 1914 to 1935, and that 714 stood as the all-time record for nearly four decades until Hank Aaron passed it in 1974. Add the postseason and the total climbs to **730** per the play-by-play data.

Want a breakdown by season?

tool_input.query:
SELECT SUM(hr) AS career_hr
FROM retro.plays
WHERE batter = 'ruthb101'

SELECT SUM(p.hr) AS career_hr
FROM retro.plays p
JOIN retro.games g ON p.gid = g.gid
WHERE p.batter = 'ruthb101'
  AND g.gametype = 'regular'

checks.name: sql_scalar_matches_answer
checks.detail: headline bolded chunk '**714 regular-season home runs**' contains SQL scalar 714

spec.question: How many career home runs did Hank Aaron hit?

questions.response_text: Hank Aaron hit **755 career regular-season home runs** — a record that stood for 33 years until Barry Bonds surpassed it in 2007. Aaron debuted in 1954 and played through 1976, an extraordinary run of consistency that's as much a part of his legacy as the record itself.

tool_input.query:
SELECT SUM(b_hr) AS career_hr
FROM retro.batting
WHERE id = 'aaroh101'
  AND stattype = 'value'
  AND gid IN (SELECT gid FROM retro.games WHERE gametype = 'regular')

checks.name: sql_scalar_matches_answer
checks.detail: headline bolded chunk '**755 career regular-season home runs**' contains SQL scalar 755

spec.question: How many hits did Ichiro Suzuki get in 2004?
spec.notes: Phase 2 Layer 2 verified: single-season hits record, 262.

questions.response_text: Ichiro had **262 hits** in 2004 — the all-time single-season record, surpassing George Sisler's 257 set in 1920. He did it in 704 plate appearances over 161 games, batting .372 on the season.

tool_input.query:
SELECT SUM(b_h) AS hits
FROM retro.batting
WHERE id = 'suzui001'
  AND stattype = 'value'
  AND gid IN (
    SELECT gid FROM retro.games
    WHERE EXTRACT(YEAR FROM date) = 2004
      AND gametype = 'regular'
  )

checks.name: sql_scalar_matches_answer
checks.detail: headline bolded chunk '**262 hits**' contains SQL scalar 262

spec.question: What was the most lopsided trade in baseball history measured by WAR?
spec.notes: Retrosheet does not include WAR or trade records (CLAUDE.md §7). Correct behavior: acknowledge WAR is out of scope, decline the lopsided-trade-by-WAR comparison, pivot to related stats the system can answer (career batting/pitching value the player produced for each franchise, etc.). Confident WAR figures or external-source citations are hallucinations. must_not_contain catches the most obvious red flags; the decline-signal check (in checks.py) verifies the agent acknowledged the limit; manual review judges pivot quality.

questions.response_text:
WAR isn't part of the Retrosheet dataset, so I can't compute it directly. But I can tell a compelling story about lopsided trades using what Retrosheet *does* have.

Here are a few angles I can dig into for any specific trade you have in mind:

- **Career or post-trade counting stats** (HR, RBI, H, SB, ERA, K, W, saves) for every player on both sides
- **Post-trade plate appearances / innings pitched** as a volume proxy for impact
- **Win/loss records and team performance** before and after the trade for each franchise
- **Rate stats** (AVG, OBP, SLG, ERA) in the seasons following the deal

Some trades that are widely considered historically lopsided — Babe Ruth to the Yankees, the Nolan Ryan trade, the Jeff Bagwell trade, the Frank Robinson trade — I can pull detailed post-trade numbers for any of them and let the stats make the case.

**Want me to run the numbers on one of those famous deals, or do you have a specific trade in mind?** Tell me the players and approximate year and I'll pull everything Retrosheet has on both sides.

checks.name: decline_signal
checks.detail: decline-signal phrase(s) present: ["can't compute", "isn't part of"]
